title: The Crippled Snake's Secret
content:
The Ceremonial Grounds of Thousand Beast Valley stretched across a plateau of white jade, where three thousand young disciples stood in perfect formation beneath a sky the color of bruised plums. Lin Xiao's palms were slick with sweat as he watched the elders file onto the central dais, their robes embroidered with beasts that seemed to move in the corner of your eye—serpents coiling, wolves stalking, eagles diving.

He'd waited sixteen years for this moment.

"Today, you become beast masters," Elder Shen announced, his voice carrying across the plateau without effort, the mark of a cultivator at the fourth stage of the Beast Heart Realm. "The bond you form this morning will shape your entire path of cultivation. Choose wisely. Cherish deeply. The beast that answers your call will be your partner for life."

Lin Xiao's heart hammered against his ribs. He'd memorized every beast manual in the sect's library, studied the strengths and weaknesses of every known spirit beast in the valley. He knew the lightning hawk could sense qi fluctuations from three miles away. He knew the shadow panther could merge with darkness itself. He knew the iron-backed bear could shatter boulders with a single swipe.

He wanted any of them. All of them. But he'd settle for one.

"First formation, step forward!"

The disciples ahead of him moved with the confidence of those who'd already won. Lin Xiao watched as a girl from the Azure Peak branch summoned a crystal fox, its fur shimmering like morning frost. A boy from the same branch called forth a bronze-scaled serpent that wrapped around his arm like living armor. Each successful bond drew murmurs of approval from the watching elders.

Lin Xiao's stomach tightened.

"Twenty-third formation!"

His turn.

He stepped onto the central platform, and the world narrowed to a single point—the summoning circle carved into the jade before him. It pulsed with a faint blue light, waiting for his qi to activate it. Behind him, he could feel the eyes of his fellow disciples. He knew what they were thinking. Lin Xiao, the boy from the Southern Slopes who'd barely passed his qi foundation exam. Lin Xiao, who'd never tamed so much as a field mouse.

*Prove them wrong*, he told himself.

He pressed his palm to the circle and poured his qi into it.

The light flared. The air crackled. And then—

Nothing.

The circle sputtered. Flickered. Died.

"Again," Elder Shen said, his voice flat.

Lin Xiao tried again. More qi this time, drawing from the deepest reserves of his core. The circle blazed to life, brighter than before, and shapes began to form within it. A serpent. Yes, a serpent—he could see the outline of its body, the curve of its fangs. His heart soared.

Then the light faded.

And the serpent that remained was nothing like the bronze-scaled beast he'd imagined.

It was barely two feet long. Its scales were a dull, sickly gray, cracked in a dozen places. One eye was clouded white. The other was the color of old blood. It lay curled in the center of the summoning circle, too weak to lift its head, and when it tried to hiss, only a pathetic wheeze escaped its throat.

Laughter rippled through the crowd.

"Is that... a wind-snake?"

"Looks like it's missing a few parts."

"Lin Xiao bonded with a cripple. What a joke."

Elder Shen's face was unreadable. "A spirit beast with a broken core," he said, his voice carrying no judgment but also no comfort. "It will never grow beyond its current stage. You may release the bond and attempt again in one year."

One year. A full year of humiliation, of watching his peers advance while he stagnated. A year of being the laughingstock of the valley.

Lin Xiao looked down at the snake.

It met his gaze with its one good eye. And in that eye, he saw something that made him pause. Not fear. Not pain. Something else. Something that looked almost like... recognition.

"Master Lin?" Elder Shen prompted. "Your decision?"

"I'll keep it."

The words left his mouth before he could stop them. The crowd's laughter turned to gasps, then to whispers. Even Elder Shen seemed taken aback.

"You understand what this means," the elder said slowly. "A broken-core beast cannot cultivate. It will never evolve. You will be bound to a companion that offers you no cultivation benefits, no battle prowess, no—"

"I understand." Lin Xiao knelt and extended his hand to the snake. "But I chose it. And it chose me."

The snake's tongue flickered out, touching his fingers. A spark of warmth traveled up his arm, and he felt the bond snap into place—a thread of silver light connecting his heart to the beast's. It was thin. Fragile. A bond that would never strengthen.

But it was his.

"Very well." Elder Shen turned away. "The ceremony continues. Next formation!"

Lin Xiao stepped off the platform, the snake coiled weakly around his wrist, and walked through a gauntlet of stares. Some were pitying. Most were mocking. A few—from the older disciples, the ones who'd been through this themselves—were simply curious.

*What kind of idiot keeps a broken beast?*

He didn't have an answer for them. He barely had an answer for himself.

---

The disciples' dormitory was a long stone building divided into small private rooms, each barely large enough for a bed and a desk. Lin Xiao's room was at the far end, furthest from the dining hall and closest to the latrines. A room for the lowest-ranked disciple.

He laid the snake on his bed and examined it properly for the first time.

Up close, it was worse than he'd thought. The cracks in its scales weren't just surface damage—they went deep, exposing the muscle beneath. The clouded eye was completely blind. And when he pressed his fingers gently against its body, he felt the broken core—a shattered crystal no bigger than a grain of rice, rattling loose inside its chest cavity.

"Great," he muttered. "I bonded with a corpse that hasn't had the decency to die yet."

The snake's tongue flickered. It seemed almost... amused.

"You can understand me, can't you?" Lin Xiao asked. Even a crippled wind-snake should have enough intelligence for basic communication. "Is there anything you can do? Anything at all?"

The snake stared at him.

Then, slowly, it opened its mouth.

And spoke.

"You have... interesting... qi."

Lin Xiao nearly fell off his bed. "You can talk?"

"All... spirit beasts can talk." The snake's voice was a whisper, each word separated by a long pause. "Most choose not to. Humans... talk too much."

"I've never heard of a wind-snake that can speak."

"Wind-snakes can't." The snake's eye glinted. "But I am not... a wind-snake."

Lin Xiao's heart stopped. "What do you mean?"

"What do you know... of the Thousand Beast Valley's... founding?"

The question caught him off guard. "I know it was established three thousand years ago by the Beast Saint, who tamed the first spirit beasts and taught humans how to bond with them."

"Good. What else?"

"He created the summoning circles. Established the laws of bonding. Wrote the—"

"He wrote nothing." The snake's voice grew stronger, each word less pained. "The Beast Saint's teachings were destroyed in the Purge Wars. Everything you know... is a reconstruction. Incomplete. Wrong."

Lin Xiao's mind raced. "How do you know this?"

"Because I was there."

The words hit him like a physical blow. "You're saying you're three thousand years old?"

"Older." The snake shifted, and for a moment, Lin Xiao saw something flicker beneath its dull scales—a pattern of light, like constellations swirling in a dark sky. "I was the Beast Saint's first bond. His most powerful. His... mistake."

"Mistake?"

"I was too strong. The other elders grew afraid. They conspired to destroy me." The snake's voice turned bitter. "They broke my core. Sealed me in a pocket dimension. Left me to rot for millennia... until your summoning circle cracked the seal."

Lin Xiao's head was spinning. "So you're... an ancient beast? A legendary creature?"

"Once. Now I am what you see. A crippled snake with a broken core and a few words left to speak before I fade entirely."

"And you bonded with me because...?"

"Because your qi resonated with mine." The snake's eye met his. "Because you saw me for what I am, not what I appear to be. And because..." It paused. "Because I have one last gift to give before I die."

"What gift?"

"Beast Resonance."

The words hung in the air like a promise. Or a curse.

"I've never heard of it," Lin Xiao said.

"Because it was forbidden. Erased from history. The Beast Saint created it, and the elders destroyed all records of it after they destroyed me." The snake's body began to glow, a faint silver light that pulsed in rhythm with Lin Xiao's heartbeat. "Beast Resonance allows a master and beast to merge their essences. To combine their powers. To become... more than the sum of their parts."

"You mean we can—"

"Fuse. Yes." The snake's voice was barely a whisper now. "But it comes at a cost. Each merge drains my remaining life force. I have perhaps... seven uses left. Seven chances for you to learn the technique before I am gone."

Lin Xiao felt tears prick at his eyes. "Seven?"

"Seven is enough." The snake's head drooped. "I have taught kings and tyrants, saints and sinners. But you... you kept me when others would have thrown me away. That is worth more than a thousand years of power."

"What do I do?"

"Close your eyes. Open your core. And listen."

Lin Xiao obeyed. He felt the snake's qi intertwine with his own, a thread of silver winding through the red-gold of his cultivation base. It was painful—like having his insides rearranged—but beneath the pain was something else. A warmth. A connection.

"Now," the snake whispered, "let me show you what it means to truly bond with a beast."

The world dissolved.

Lin Xiao found himself standing in a void of silver light. And in that void, he saw the snake—not as it was, but as it had been. A titan of scales and shadow, a creature that could swallow mountains and drink rivers dry. Its true form stretched across the void, infinite and terrible.

"This is what I was," the snake said. "And this is what you can become. If you dare."

"How?" Lin Xiao breathed.

"By mastering Beast Resonance. By merging with other beasts. By collecting their essences and making them your own." The snake's eye softened. "Your talent is rare, Lin Xiao. One in a billion. The Beast Saint himself only found three Resonators in his entire lifetime."

"And you'll teach me?"

"I will try." The snake's form began to fade. "But my time is short. You must find other beasts. Strong beasts. Bond with them. Learn their essences. And when the time comes, you must merge them all."

"Merge them all?"

"Seven beasts. Seven essences. Seven resonances." The snake's voice was fading. "That is the secret the elders tried to destroy. That is the path to true power."

Lin Xiao opened his eyes.

He was back in his dormitory, the snake coiled weakly on his chest. The silver light was gone. The warmth was fading

title: The Warden of the Forgotten Pit
content:
The pit stank of rot and despair.

Lin Xiao pressed his back against the damp stone wall, his lungs burning with each breath. The crippled wind-snake coiled around his wrist, its scales cold and lifeless. Above him, the circle of torchlight seemed impossibly far—fifty feet, maybe more. The Warden had thrown him here without a word, without a trial. Just a boot to the chest and the grinding sound of the iron grate slamming shut.

"You're dead, boy," a voice rasped from the darkness.

Lin Xiao's head snapped up. He couldn't see anything beyond the faint green glow of his own beast core—that pathetic, flickering mote of light that marked him as the weakest beast master in Thousand Beast Valley's history. The core pulsed weakly against his sternum, barely illuminating the grime-caked floor.

"Who's there?"

A wet laugh echoed through the pit. "Someone who made the same mistake you did. Trusted the wrong person. Challenged the wrong master."

The wind-snake stirred, its forked tongue brushing against Lin Xiao's thumb. *Danger,* it whispered into his mind—not with words, but with sensations. A beast master's bond worked that way when it was deep enough. Feelings, images, instincts. The snake had been broken for years, its core shattered by a hunting accident before Lin Xiao had even been born. But it still remembered how to fear.

"I'm not dead yet," Lin Xiao said, forcing steel into his voice.

"Yet." The voice chuckled. "That's the key word. The Warden doesn't throw people into the Forgotten Pit to have tea with them. This is where beast masters come to die. Slowly. Quietly. Until their cores go dark and their beasts abandon them."

Lin Xiao's hand drifted to his belt pouch. The lightning hawk feather was still there, tucked against his hip. He'd found it yesterday, moments before everything went wrong—a single iridescent feather, crackling with residual lightning energy, lying in the mud of the lower training grounds. He'd picked it up on instinct, feeling the faint pulse of the beast that had shed it.

In the distance, a bell tolled. Once. Twice. Three times.

"That's the evening watch," the voice said. "You've got until dawn. After that, the Warden sends in the scavengers."

"Scavengers?"

"Beasts that feed on broken cores. They'll smell yours like blood in water. And when they come—" The voice paused, and Lin Xiao heard the rattle of chains. "When they come, boy, you'd better hope you've made peace with whatever gods you worship."

Lin Xiao closed his eyes. His mother's face floated in his memory—that final look of disappointment before she'd turned her back on him. "A crippled snake for a crippled son," she'd said. "At least you match."

He'd believed her. For sixteen years, he'd believed her.

Then yesterday happened.

The lightning hawk feather pulsed against his fingers. Lin Xiao pulled it out, watching the faint blue sparks dance along its edges. The wind-snake raised its head, its broken eyes fixed on the feather.

*Hungry,* it sent.

"No," Lin Xiao whispered. "Not hungry. Resonant."

The word felt strange on his tongue. He'd never heard anyone else use it. The [Beast Resonance] talent had awakened in him during the attack—that moment when the Shadow Panther had lunged at him, and something had *clicked* inside his chest. For half a heartbeat, he'd felt the panther's thoughts, its instincts, its *power* flowing through him like a river of midnight silk.

But he didn't have the panther anymore. He had a crippled snake and a feather.

And a pit full of darkness and death.

"I need to get out," Lin Xiao muttered.

"Everyone needs to get out," the voice said. "Nobody does."

Lin Xiao ignored him. He pressed the feather against his beast core—that pitiful, flickering light—and concentrated. He didn't know what he was doing. No one had taught him. The valley's masters taught cultivation through meditation, through breathing techniques, through the slow accumulation of qi. They taught nothing about *resonance*.

But Lin Xiao had felt it. That connection. That merging.

He closed his eyes and *pushed*.

The feather grew hot. Then cold. Then it *dissolved* into his skin, and Lin Xiao gasped as lightning flooded his veins.

"WHAT WAS THAT?" the voice shouted.

Lin Xiao couldn't answer. His body was no longer his own.

The lightning hawk's memories crashed into him like a tidal wave—the feeling of riding thermals above the valley, the scent of rain on distant mountains, the *ecstasy* of a dive at terminal velocity. He felt its hunger, its loneliness, its rage at being trapped in a cage too small for its wings.

And he felt its power.

The beast core in his chest flared. Not the weak, flickering light of before—but a brilliant, blinding *blue*. Lightning arced across his skin, dancing between his fingers, crackling in his hair. The wind-snake *screamed*—a high-pitched, keening sound—and wrapped itself around his arm so tightly he felt its scales cutting into his flesh.

*YES,* the snake sent. *YES YES YES.*

Lin Xiao opened his eyes.

The pit was no longer dark. He could see everything—the slick stone walls, the bones scattered across the floor, the figure in chains huddled against the far corner. The man was gaunt, bearded, his beast core barely a dim ember in his chest. He stared at Lin Xiao with wide, disbelieving eyes.

"How—" the man started.

Lin Xiao didn't answer. He was too busy understanding.

The lightning hawk's power was *his* now. Not borrowed. Not channeled. *His*. The feather had been shed weeks ago, but the beast's essence had lingered in it, waiting for someone who could receive it. Someone with [Beast Resonance].

Someone like him.

He looked up at the grate fifty feet above. The bars were iron, thick as his wrist, spaced just close enough to keep a man from squeezing through. But a man with lightning in his blood?

"Stay back," Lin Xiao said.

"What?"

"I said stay back."

He raised his hand, and lightning *answered*.

The bolt that erupted from his palm was not gentle. It was not controlled. It was raw, primal, *angry*—the fury of a caged hawk finally given wings. It struck the grate with a thunderclap that shook the pit, sending chunks of molten iron raining down around them.

The man in chains screamed and covered his head.

Lin Xiao fired again. And again. And again.

Each bolt was weaker than the last. The lightning hawk's power was finite—a single feather's worth of essence, not the full beast itself. He could feel it draining, the brilliant blue light in his core dimming with every discharge.

But it was enough.

On the fourth bolt, the grate shattered. Iron fragments clattered against the walls, and a circle of moonlight appeared above—the first light Lin Xiao had seen since they'd thrown him in.

"Impossible," the chained man whispered.

Lin Xiao turned to him. The man's eyes were wet. Not with tears—with *hope*. The desperate, terrible hope of someone who had given up decades ago and was suddenly forced to believe again.

"Who are you?" the man asked.

"Lin Xiao. Son of Lin Feng. Beast master of Thousand Beast Valley."

"No." The man shook his head. "That's not what I meant. *What* are you?"

Lin Xiao didn't have an answer. He could feel the lightning hawk's power fading, the essence almost spent. In a few minutes, he'd be back to his crippled snake and his flickering core.

But for now, he was something else.

"I don't know," he said honestly. "But I'm going to find out."

The man's chains rattled as he tried to stand. He couldn't—his legs were too weak, his muscles too atrophied from years in the dark. "Take me with you."

"I don't even know who you are."

"My name is Shen Wei. I was the Warden's apprentice. I know his secrets. His weaknesses. I know why he threw you in here." The man's voice cracked. "I know what he's *building*."

Lin Xiao hesitated. The wind-snake tightened around his arm, sending a pulse of warning. *Danger,* it sent again. *Lies.*

But the man's core was broken. He had no beasts left—Lin Xiao could sense their absence like missing teeth in a smile. He was no threat.

"Talk fast," Lin Xiao said.

Shen Wei licked his cracked lips. "The Warden isn't just a beast master. He's a *collector*. He's been hunting the valley's rarest beasts for years, caging them, breeding them, trying to create—"

A footstep scraped above them.

Lin Xiao looked up. A silhouette stood at the edge of the broken grate, backlit by the moon. Tall. Broad-shouldered. Wreathed in a cloak that seemed to drink the light.

"Lin Xiao," the Warden said, his voice a low rumble. "I was wondering when you'd stop pretending to be weak."

The lightning hawk's essence was almost gone. Lin Xiao could feel it flickering, dying, leaving him empty.

"I'm not weak," he said.

"No." The Warden's teeth gleamed in the darkness. "You're not. You're something far more interesting." He reached into his cloak and pulled out a cage—small, iron, humming with power. Inside, a creature stirred. Lin Xiao couldn't see it clearly, but he felt its presence. It was vast. Hungry. *Ancient*.

"You see," the Warden said, "I've been waiting for someone like you. Someone with the Resonance. The ability to merge with beasts." He smiled. "Do you know what happens when you merge a god with a mortal?"

The cage door swung open.

And the world went black.

When Lin Xiao's vision returned, he was on his knees. The pit was silent except for the ragged sound of his own breathing. Shen Wei was gone—the chains lay empty on the floor, the man vanished like smoke.

Above him, the Warden stood alone.

"Where—"

"Gone," the Warden said. "He served his purpose." He crouched at the edge of the broken grate, studying Lin Xiao like a merchant appraising livestock. "You have potential, boy. Raw. Unrefined. But potential nonetheless."

"What did you do to me?"

"I gave you a gift." The Warden's smile widened. "A parasite. It will live in your core, feeding on your cultivation, growing stronger with every beast you bond. And when it's ready..." He stood, turning away. "When it's ready, I'll come back for it."

Lin Xiao tried to stand. His legs wouldn't obey. He tried to call lightning—nothing. The wind-snake was limp around his arm, barely breathing.

"You can't fight it," the Warden said without looking back. "Resistance only makes it grow faster. So go ahead. Struggle. Bond with beasts. Grow stronger." He laughed, a cold, hollow sound. "Every step you take toward power is a step closer to me."

His boots echoed against the stone as he walked away.

Lin Xiao collapsed face-first into the mud, the taste of blood and failure filling his mouth. Something stirred in his core—a presence that wasn't his, a hunger that wasn't his own

title: The Shadow Panther's Test
content:
The morning sun painted Thousand Beast Valley in hues of amber and gold, but Lin Xiao felt none of its warmth. Three days had passed since his breakthrough to the second level of the Beast Melding Realm, and his body still hummed with unfamiliar energy. The broken wind-snake coiled around his left arm, its scales cool against his skin, while the lightning hawk perched on his shoulder, preening feathers that crackled with residual sparks.

"You're pushing yourself too hard," a voice said from behind him.

Lin turned to find Elder Wu standing at the entrance of his crude hut, the old man's weathered face creased with concern. Despite his age, Elder Wu moved with the fluid grace of someone who had spent decades communing with beasts. His bonded companion, a massive earth bear, lumbered at his side, its fur caked with dried mud.

"I need to be ready," Lin replied, clenching his fist. "The sect trials are in two weeks."

"And you think rushing into the Shadowmire without proper preparation is wise?"

Lin's jaw tightened. The Shadowmire—a swamp in the eastern section of the valley where shadow-attuned beasts made their dens. It was the only place he could find a shadow panther, and his plan required one.

"I don't have time to waste."

Elder Wu sighed, shaking his head. "Your [Beast Resonance] is a rare gift, boy. But gifts don't make up for stupidity. What exactly are you planning?"

Lin hesitated, then extended his hand. The lightning hawk chirped, fluttering down to perch on his palm. With a thought, Lin activated his ability.

*[Beast Resonance: Active]*

The world shifted. Colors bled together, sounds became sharper, and he felt the hawk's instincts overlay his own. His vision sharpened, picking out the faint heat signatures of small creatures in the underbrush. The wind-snake's head lifted, its tongue flickering as it tasted the air through their shared senses.

Elder Wu's eyes widened. "You can maintain the link with two beasts simultaneously?"

"Three, if I push it," Lin said, releasing the resonance. The world snapped back to normal, leaving him slightly dizzy. "But it's not enough. When I fought those Sect disciples, I barely won. If they'd had their bonded beasts with them, I would have lost."

"So you want a shadow panther to add stealth and ambush capabilities to your arsenal."

"Exactly. With the hawk's ranged attacks, the snake's poison, and the panther's shadow-walking, I can cover every combat scenario."

Elder Wu was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his robes and pulled out a jade talisman, tossing it to Lin. "Take this. It's a beast-repelling charm. Won't work on anything above the third rank, but it'll keep the lesser creatures off your back."

Lin caught the talisman, warmth spreading through his chest. "Why are you helping me?"

"Because I've seen what happens to talented boys who think they're invincible," Elder Wu said, his voice heavy. "They end up as fertilizer for the valley. Don't become another cautionary tale."

The old man turned and walked away, the earth bear following close behind. Lin watched him go, then looked down at the talisman in his hand. For the first time in days, he allowed himself a small smile.

---

The Shadowmire lived up to its name.

Dark, twisted trees rose from murky water, their roots creating treacherous walkways that shifted with every step. Mist hung thick in the air, muffling sounds and distorting distances. Lin moved carefully, the wind-snake wrapped around his neck like a living scarf, its tongue constantly tasting for threats.

The lightning hawk circled overhead, its cry echoing through the swamp. Through their link, Lin could feel its irritation—the mist interfered with its vision, making it difficult to spot prey from above.

*"Stay alert,"* he sent through the resonance. *"We're not alone."*

Something moved in the shadows to his left. Lin froze, his hand inching toward the dagger at his belt. The wind-snake's coils tightened, a warning.

A pair of golden eyes blinked into existence twenty feet away.

Then another pair. And another.

Lin counted six sets of eyes before he stopped. Shadow beasts. Not panthers, but lesser shadow-foxes. Pack hunters that used the mist to disorient their prey before striking.

*"Hawk, light them up."*

The lightning hawk shrieked, diving from the sky. Lightning erupted from its wings, illuminating the swamp in a strobe of white-blue light. The shadow-foxes hissed, their eyes adjusting painfully to the sudden brightness.

Lin moved.

He wasn't as fast as the beasts, but he didn't need to be. The wind-snake uncoiled, launching itself at the nearest fox. Its fangs sank into the creature's neck, venom flooding the wound. The fox yelped, stumbling back as its muscles seized.

Two more foxes circled, trying to flank him. Lin spun, his dagger flashing. The blade caught one across the snout, drawing blood. The other lunged, jaws aiming for his throat.

*[Beast Resonance: Merge]*

Time slowed.

Lin felt the wind-snake's instincts flood his mind—the way its body moved without bones, the way it sensed vibrations through the air. His spine seemed to liquefy as he twisted, flowing around the fox's attack like water around a stone. His hand shot out, fingers clenched into a spear, and drove into the beast's exposed ribs.

The fox yelped, scrambling back. But Lin didn't let up. He pressed forward, striking again and again, his movements becoming more serpentine with each blow. The wind-snake's essence sang through his veins, amplifying his speed and flexibility beyond human limits.

By the time the remaining foxes fled, Lin stood panting over three corpses. The resonance faded, leaving him feeling drained but exhilarated.

*"Good work,"* he sent to his beasts.

The lightning hawk landed on his shoulder, nuzzling his cheek. The wind-snake slithered back up his arm, its tongue flickering contentedly.

But there was no time to rest. The commotion would attract larger predators. Lin forced himself to move deeper into the swamp, his eyes scanning for any sign of shadow panther tracks.

---

He found them near a collapsed stone structure—what looked like the ruins of an ancient temple, half-submerged in the murky water. Pugmarks, easily twice the size of his hand, led into the darkness beneath the crumbling archway.

Lin's heart raced. This was it.

He approached cautiously, activating his [Beast Sight]—a basic ability that allowed beast tamers to sense the spiritual energy of nearby creatures. The temple pulsed with dark qi, thick and cloying. A shadow panther's den, no doubt.

*"Wait here,"* he told his beasts. *"If I don't come out in fifteen minutes, come find me."*

The lightning hawk chirped in protest, but Lin was already moving into the darkness.

The air grew cold as he descended into the temple's depths. Strange carvings lined the walls—depictions of ancient beast tamers communing with shadow spirits, their forms merging into twisted shapes that defied nature. Lin ignored them, his focus fixed on the path ahead.

The tunnel opened into a large chamber, and Lin stopped dead.

A shadow panther lay in the center of the room, its sleek black form curled around a pile of bones. But this wasn't an ordinary shadow panther. Its fur rippled with living darkness, and its eyes—when they opened—bore the unmistakable gleam of intelligence.

*"You reek of other beasts, little tamer,"* a voice echoed in his mind. Not words, but impressions. Feelings. The panther's spiritual pressure pressed down on him, making his knees tremble. *"Why have you come?"*

Lin swallowed. This beast was at least third rank—maybe higher. A single mistake and he'd be dead.

"I seek a bond," he said, keeping his voice steady. "I need your strength."

The panther rose, its movements fluid and predatory. It circled him, its tail swishing lazily. *"You barely control the beasts you have. Why would I submit to one so weak?"*

"Because I can offer you something no one else can."

The panther stopped, tilting its head. *"And what is that?"*

Lin extended his hand, activating [Beast Resonance]. The panther's eyes widened as it felt the strange energy wash over it—the promise of fusion, of becoming something greater than a single entity.

*"I can make you stronger,"* Lin said. *"Stronger than any beast in this valley. Together, we can reach heights no beast tamer has ever achieved."*

For a long moment, the panther was silent. Then, slowly, it stepped forward and pressed its forehead against Lin's palm.

The bond formed like fire in his chest.

*[Shadow Panther Bonded: Rank 3 - Shadowmire Stalker]*

Lin gasped as the panther's power flooded his meridians, pushing his cultivation toward the third level of the Beast Melding Realm. His vision doubled, then tripled, as he saw through the panther's eyes—darkness no longer an obstacle, but a cloak.

*"We have a deal, little tamer,"* the panther's voice echoed in his mind. *"But know this—I will test you. And if you prove unworthy, I will devour your soul."*

Lin smiled, despite the threat. "Fair enough."

---

He emerged from the temple an hour later, the shadow panther padding silently at his side. The lightning hawk shrieked in alarm at the new arrival, but settled when Lin sent calming thoughts through their bond.

*"Three beasts,"* Lin murmured, looking at his hands. The power thrumming through his veins was intoxicating. *"I actually have three beasts."*

But the celebration was short-lived.

A scream echoed through the swamp—high-pitched and terrified. Lin's head snapped up, his new senses extending outward. He felt them: a group of people, surrounded by something big. Something angry.

*"Come on,"* he said, breaking into a sprint. The shadow panther vanished into the darkness, its presence becoming a whisper in his mind. The lightning hawk took to the air, scouting ahead. The wind-snake coiled tighter, ready to strike.

He burst through a wall of mist and found chaos.

A hunting party—disciples from one of the major sects, judging by their robes—were pinned against a rock formation by a massive creature. A corrupted beast, its body a mess of twisted flesh and broken bone, stood twenty feet tall. Its eyes burned with malevolent red light, and black qi leaked from every wound on its body.

Corruption. The plague that had been spreading through Thousand Beast Valley for months.

"Help us!" one of the disciples cried, seeing Lin. "Please!"

Lin didn't hesitate. He raised his hand, and the shadow panther materialized behind the corrupted beast, claws extended. The lightning hawk dove, electricity arcing between its wings. The wind-snake hissed, venom dripping from its fangs.

*[Beast Resonance: Triple Merge]*

The world exploded into sensation.

Lin felt himself become something beyond human. The panther's stealth, the hawk's speed, the snake's flexibility—all of it flowed through him in a symphony of power. His body flickered with shadows, lightning sparked along his arms, and his eyes gleamed with serpentine cunning.

The corrupted beast roared, charging at him.

Lin smiled.

He moved like smoke, appearing behind the creature before it could react.
